heading: Register your school
body: One sign-up gives your whole school access. No per-teacher or per-student account is needed to start. When you register, you create a school record and become its first School Admin. Your school gets a unique enrolment code that teachers and students use to associate their accounts with your institution. You can add more admins later by promoting any provisioned teacher. Go to: studybuddy.app → Register your school. Fill in: school name, contact email, country, and a password (12+ characters). You will receive an access token immediately and land on the school dashboard.

heading: Provision teachers
body: Add your teaching staff by email. They receive a default password and are prompted to reset it on first login. Provisioned teachers belong exclusively to your school. You set their name and email; the system generates a secure temporary password and emails it to them. On first login they are required to set a permanent password before accessing any portal pages. You can assign teachers to specific grades and promote any teacher to School Admin. Actions: Add a teacher (School portal → Teachers), Reset a teacher's password (Teachers → teacher detail), Assign grades to a teacher (Teachers → teacher detail), Promote to School Admin (Teachers → teacher detail).

heading: Enrol students
body: Upload a roster or add students individually by email and grade. Assign each student to a teacher and classroom. Students are provisioned the same way as teachers — you provide the email and grade, the system generates a temporary password. Students are then assigned to a teacher and a grade. Enrolment in a classroom determines which curriculum content they see. A student can only be enrolled in one school at a time. Go to: School portal → Students → Add student. Enter name, email, and grade. Optionally assign to a teacher immediately.

heading: Build a custom curriculum
body: Define subjects and units, submit for review, then trigger an AI-generated content build — lessons, quizzes, tutorials, and audio — in any supported language. Steps: 1. Submit a Curriculum Definition — a structured list of subjects and units. 2. A platform reviewer approves the definition (typically within 24 hours). 3. You trigger the AI content build. A cost estimate is shown before you confirm. 4. The pipeline generates content in the background (15–60 minutes per grade). 5. The finished curriculum package appears in your catalog, ready to assign. Build costs depend on your subscription plan. Starter plans include a fixed number of builds per month.

heading: Assign content to classrooms
body: Attach curriculum packages to classrooms. Students see only the content assigned to their classroom — nothing else. A classroom is the unit of content delivery. Each classroom has an optional grade and teacher. You assign one or more curriculum packages to a classroom; the packages are ordered and presented to students in that order. Platform packages (pre-built) and your own school-built packages can be mixed freely. Actions: Create a classroom (an empty container with a name, optional grade and teacher), Assign a curriculum package (students in the classroom can access that package content), Reorder packages (controls the sequence students work through content), Enrol a student (student gains access to all packages assigned to the classroom), Archive a classroom (classroom hidden from active views; data preserved).

heading: Track progress and at-risk students
body: Per-student completion rates, quiz scores, and an at-risk flag for students falling behind — visible to both admins and teachers. StudyBuddy tracks every lesson view, quiz attempt, and session duration for each student. School Admins see aggregate metrics across the whole school. Teachers see metrics for their assigned students. An at-risk flag is raised automatically when a student's activity falls below their class average for seven consecutive days. Admins can download class-level and school-level reports as CSV. Reports: Class overview (completion rate, quiz average, streak, last activity per student), At-risk students (flagged students with days since last activity), Unit performance (average quiz score per unit), School summary (aggregated metrics across all classrooms).

heading: Step 1: Set your permanent password
body: After registering your school you arrive at the school portal. If you were provisioned by someone else (not a self-registration), you must set a permanent password before you can access any other page. Go to: School portal → the forced password change page. Enter your current temporary password, choose a new password (minimum 12 characters), confirm it, and click Save password. You will not be able to navigate to any other portal page until this step is complete.

heading: Step 2: Add your first teacher
body: Go to School portal → Teachers → Add teacher. Enter the teacher's full name and email address. Click Add teacher. The system creates the account and emails a temporary password to the teacher. The teacher must log in and reset their password before they can access any pages. You can assign grades to the teacher on the same page.

heading: Step 3: Enrol your first students
body: Go to School portal → Students → Add student. Enter the student's full name, email address, and grade (1–12). Optionally assign a teacher. Click Add student. Repeat for each student, or upload a roster CSV. The system emails a temporary password to each student. Students must log in and reset their passwords on first login.

heading: Step 4: Create a classroom
body: Go to School portal → Classrooms → Create classroom. Enter a classroom name (e.g. Grade 8 — Section A), optionally select a grade and teacher. Click Create. The classroom is created empty. Assign curriculum packages to it next, then enrol students.

heading: Step 5: Assign a curriculum to the classroom
body: Go to School portal → Classrooms → click your classroom → Assign package. Browse the catalog or enter a curriculum ID. Click Assign. Students enrolled in this classroom will now have access to the assigned curriculum content. You can assign multiple packages and reorder them.

heading: Provisioning a teacher
body: To add a teacher: go to School portal → Teachers → Add teacher. Enter the teacher's full name and email. The system generates a temporary password and emails it. The teacher logs in with their email and that temporary password. On first login they are forced to set a new permanent password. You can assign the teacher to one or more grades. Teachers only see students and reports for their assigned grades.

heading: Reset a teacher's password
body: To reset a teacher's password: go to School portal → Teachers → click the teacher's name → Reset password. A new temporary password is generated and emailed to the teacher. The teacher must log in and set a new permanent password on their next login. You cannot view or set the password directly — only reset it to a new temporary one.

heading: Promote a teacher to School Admin
body: To promote a teacher to School Admin: go to School portal → Teachers → click the teacher's name → Promote to admin. The teacher's role changes to school_admin. They gain all admin capabilities: provisioning teachers and students, managing classrooms, approving curricula, and viewing school-wide reports. Promotion cannot be undone from the UI — contact support if you need to demote an admin.

heading: Provisioning a student
body: To add a student: go to School portal → Students → Add student. Enter full name, email address, and grade (1–12). Optionally assign a teacher. Click Add student. The system emails a temporary password to the student. The student must log in with their email and that temporary password, then set a new permanent password on first login. Students under 13 require parental consent before their account becomes active.

heading: Assigning a student to a teacher
body: To assign a student to a teacher: go to School portal → Students → click the student's name → Assignment. Select the teacher from the dropdown. Click Save. The teacher will now see this student in their reports and at-risk dashboard. A student can only be assigned to one teacher at a time. To reassign, return to the same page and select a different teacher. Grade changes for school-enrolled students must be made here — students cannot change their own grade.

heading: Enrolling a student in a classroom
body: To enrol a student in a classroom: go to School portal → Classrooms → click the classroom → Students section → Enrol student. Enter the student's name or ID. Click Enrol. The student now has access to all curriculum packages assigned to this classroom. A student can be enrolled in multiple classrooms. Removing a student from a classroom revokes their access to that classroom's content but does not delete their progress data.

heading: Creating a classroom
body: To create a classroom: go to School portal → Classrooms → Create classroom. Enter a classroom name (required), optionally select a grade (1–12) and assign a teacher. Click Create. The classroom is created with status active and no packages or students. Next steps: assign a curriculum package, then enrol students. A classroom with no packages assigned shows no content to enrolled students.

heading: Assigning a curriculum package to a classroom
body: To assign a curriculum package: go to School portal → Classrooms → click the classroom → Assign package. Browse the catalog or search by curriculum name. Click Assign next to the package you want. Students enrolled in the classroom immediately gain access to that package's content. You can assign multiple packages. Use the reorder handles to change the sequence students see them. Remove a package by clicking the X next to it — this revokes student access to that package's content.

heading: Archiving and restoring a classroom
body: To archive a classroom: go to School portal → Classrooms → click the classroom → Archive. Archived classrooms are hidden from the active classroom list but all data (students, packages, progress) is preserved. To restore: go to Classrooms → Archived tab → Restore. Students in archived classrooms lose access to the classroom's content while archived. Archiving does not delete any progress or assignment data.

heading: Browsing the curriculum catalog
body: To browse available curriculum packages: go to School portal → Catalog. Filter by grade using the Grade dropdown. Each package shows the grade level, subjects covered, and a readiness indicator. A green bar means all content (lessons, quizzes, audio) has been generated for that subject. A yellow bar means content is partially generated. To assign a package directly from the catalog: click Assign to classroom and select the classroom.

heading: Submitting a curriculum definition
body: To build a custom curriculum: go to School portal → Curriculum → New definition. Step 1: enter the curriculum name and select a grade. Step 2: add subjects. For each subject, add unit titles. Step 3: select the languages to generate content in (English is always included). Step 4: review and submit. The definition status becomes pending_approval. A platform reviewer will approve or reject it, typically within 24 hours. You will receive an email when the status changes.

heading: Triggering a curriculum build
body: After your curriculum definition is approved: go to School portal → Curriculum → Definitions → click your definition → Trigger build. A cost estimate is shown: number of unit runs, estimated tokens, and the charge amount if your build allowance is exhausted. Click Confirm to start the build. The pipeline runs in the background (15–60 minutes per grade). Monitor progress at School portal → Curriculum → Jobs. The job shows status (queued, running, complete, failed) and a progress bar.

heading: Subscription plans
body: StudyBuddy OnDemand offers three school subscription plans: Starter (up to 50 students, 10 teachers, 1 curriculum build per year), Professional (up to 200 students, 50 teachers, 3 curriculum builds per year), Enterprise (unlimited students and teachers, unlimited builds). All plans include: platform catalog access, classroom management, teacher and student provisioning, progress reports, and CSV export. Upgrade or downgrade at any time — changes take effect on the next billing cycle.

heading: Extra curriculum builds
body: If your plan's build allowance is exhausted, you can purchase additional builds individually. Each extra build costs a flat fee (shown in the cost estimate before you confirm). The charge is made immediately to your card on file via Stripe. Extra builds do not roll over to the next subscription year — they expire with the current plan year. Build allowance is tracked per school subscription year, not per calendar month.

heading: Create and manage classrooms
body: Classrooms are containers for content delivery. Create a classroom, attach curriculum packages, and enrol students. Each student sees only the content assigned to their classroom. Steps: 1. Go to School portal → Classrooms → Create classroom. 2. Enter a classroom name and select your grade. 3. Click Create. 4. Click Assign package to attach curriculum content. 5. Go to the Students tab to enrol students. Students enrolled in the classroom immediately gain access to all assigned packages. See help/teacher/classrooms.html for detailed steps.

heading: View student progress reports
body: Reports show per-student activity across all your classrooms. You see completion rates, quiz averages, streak counts, and last-active dates. Reports are scoped to students assigned to you only. Steps: 1. Go to School portal → Reports. 2. The Overview tab shows one row per student: completion rate, quiz average, streak, last activity. 3. Click Download CSV to export any report. Available tabs: Overview, At-risk (students inactive 7+ days), Unit performance (average quiz score per unit). See help/teacher/reports.html for all report types.

heading: At-risk students overview
body: The platform automatically flags a student as at-risk when they have had no activity for seven or more consecutive days. An at-risk flag does not prevent the student from studying — it is an alert for you to follow up. Steps: 1. Go to School portal → Reports → At-risk tab. 2. The at-risk list shows each flagged student, their days since last activity, and their classroom. 3. Use this list to prioritise follow-up conversations with students or their families. The at-risk flag is automatically cleared once the student completes any lesson or quiz.

heading: Your first login as a teacher
body: Your School Admin creates your account and emails you a temporary password. When you log in for the first time you are taken to a Change Password screen. You cannot access any other page until you set a permanent password (minimum 12 characters). Steps: 1. Open the school portal URL and click Log in. 2. Enter your email and the temporary password from the email. 3. On the Change Password screen, enter your temporary password, choose a new one, and click Save password. 4. You are now logged in and can access your classrooms and reports. If you forget your password later, click Forgot password on the login page — a reset link is sent to your email without needing to contact your admin.

heading: Create a classroom
body: A classroom is an empty container until you assign packages and enrol students. Steps: 1. Go to School portal → Classrooms. 2. Click Create classroom (top right). 3. Enter a classroom name (e.g. Grade 8 — Section A). 4. Optionally select a grade (1–12). 5. Click Create. The classroom is created with status Active and zero packages and students. A classroom with no packages assigned shows no content to enrolled students — always assign at least one curriculum package before enrolling students.

heading: Assign a curriculum package to a classroom
body: Curriculum packages contain the lessons, quizzes, tutorials, and audio for a subject at a given grade level. You can assign multiple packages and control the order students see them. Steps: 1. Go to School portal → Classrooms → click your classroom. 2. Click Assign package. 3. Browse the catalog or search by curriculum name. Platform packages and your school's own packages are both listed. 4. Click Assign next to the package you want. 5. To reorder packages, drag the handles on the package list — students work through them in that order. Students enrolled in this classroom immediately gain access to the package content.

heading: Enrol students in a classroom
body: You can only enrol students who are already assigned to you by your School Admin. A student can be enrolled in more than one classroom. Steps: 1. Go to School portal → Classrooms → click your classroom. 2. Click the Students tab. 3. Click Enrol student. 4. Search for the student by name or email. 5. Click Enrol to confirm. The student now has access to all curriculum packages assigned to this classroom. If a student you expect is not in search results, they may not be assigned to you yet — ask your School Admin to assign the student to you via School portal → Students → Assignment.

heading: Remove a student from a classroom
body: Removing a student revokes their access to the classroom's content. Their progress data is preserved — if you re-enrol them later, their history is intact. Steps: 1. Go to School portal → Classrooms → click your classroom. 2. Click the Students tab. 3. Find the student and click Remove next to their name. 4. Confirm the action in the dialog. The student no longer has access to this classroom's content but progress data is retained for your records.

heading: Archive and restore a classroom
body: Archive a classroom at the end of a term to keep your active list tidy. Archived classrooms are hidden from the main view but all data is preserved. Steps to archive: 1. Go to School portal → Classrooms → click your classroom. 2. Click Archive (bottom of the classroom settings panel). 3. Confirm the action. The classroom moves to the Archived tab. Students lose access to its content while it is archived. No progress data is deleted. To restore: go to Classrooms → Archived tab → click Restore.

heading: Class overview report
body: The overview report gives you a row per student showing their overall progress across all enrolled classrooms. Steps: 1. Go to School portal → Reports. 2. The Overview tab is selected by default. 3. Each row shows: student name, classroom, completion rate (%), quiz average (%), current streak (days), and last active date. 4. Click any column header to sort. Click again to reverse the sort. This report is useful for a quick weekly health check on all your students.

heading: At-risk student report
body: The at-risk report lists every student who has not been active for seven or more consecutive days. Students are flagged automatically. Steps: 1. Go to School portal → Reports → At-risk tab. 2. Each row shows: student name, classroom, days since last activity, and current completion rate. 3. Prioritise students with the most days inactive and lowest completion rates. 4. Once a student completes any lesson or quiz, the at-risk flag clears automatically — you do not need to manually reset it. At-risk flags update nightly. The flag is a soft alert only — it does not restrict the student's access to content.

heading: Unit performance report
body: The unit performance report shows average quiz scores per unit across your students. Use it to identify curriculum units where students are consistently struggling. Steps: 1. Go to School portal → Reports → Unit performance tab. 2. Select a classroom from the dropdown to filter. 3. Each row shows: unit name, subject, number of attempts, and average best score (%). Units with average scores below 60% are highlighted in amber as a signal to review that material in class. Low-scoring units are useful for planning extra classroom time on specific topics.

heading: Download a CSV report
body: All reports can be exported as CSV files for use in a spreadsheet, grade book, or to share with your School Admin. Steps: 1. Go to School portal → Reports. 2. Open the tab for the report you want: Overview, At-risk, or Unit performance. 3. Click Download CSV (top right of the report table). 4. The file downloads immediately to your browser's default download folder. The CSV contains the same columns shown on screen, with one row per student (or per unit, for the unit performance report).

heading: Understanding student report metrics
body: Metric definitions for the student reports. Completion rate: percentage of units in assigned packages that the student has opened at least once — opening a unit counts as a view. Quiz average: average of each student's best score per quiz across all quizzes in their enrolled classrooms; multiple attempts are allowed and only the best attempt counts. Streak: number of consecutive calendar days the student has been active (opened at least one lesson or submitted at least one quiz answer); resets to 0 if a full calendar day passes with no activity. Last active: the most recent date on which the student had any recorded activity (lesson view, quiz attempt, or tutorial session).

heading: View your students
body: The Students page lists every student assigned to you by your School Admin. Your School Admin controls which students belong to your roster — you cannot add or remove students from your roster yourself. Steps: 1. Go to School portal → Students. 2. The list shows each student's name, grade, enrollment status, and classrooms they are enrolled in. 3. Use the search bar to find a student by name. 4. Click a student's name to see their full detail page. You see only your assigned students. If a student you expect is missing, ask your School Admin to assign them to you via School portal → Students → the student's name → Assignment.

heading: Student progress detail
body: Clicking a student's name opens their detail page with a full breakdown of their activity. Steps: 1. Go to School portal → Students → click the student's name. 2. The Overview section shows their completion rate, quiz average, current streak, and last active date. 3. The Classrooms section shows their progress per classroom: which packages they have started, how far they are through each unit, and quiz scores per unit. 4. The Activity log section shows a chronological list of every lesson view, quiz attempt, and session in the last 30 days. This page gives you the full picture of a student's engagement history.

heading: Responding to at-risk students
body: When a student is flagged at-risk (no activity for 7+ days), the most effective response is direct personal outreach. The platform does not send automated emails to students — that follow-up is your responsibility. Steps: 1. Go to School portal → Reports → At-risk tab to see all flagged students. 2. Click a student's name to open their progress detail page to understand why they may have stopped — check which unit they were last on and their most recent quiz score. 3. Contact the student or their family through your school's normal communication channel. 4. Consider whether the student is struggling with specific content: the Unit performance report can reveal if their lowest-scored units correlate with when they stopped logging in. Once the student completes any lesson or quiz, the at-risk flag clears automatically.

heading: What content can a student access?
body: A student's content access is determined entirely by their classroom enrolments. They see only the curriculum packages assigned to the classrooms they are enrolled in. Content access by student state: Enrolled in a classroom with packages → all lessons, quizzes, tutorials, and audio for the assigned packages. Enrolled in a classroom with no packages → an empty content page until you assign a package. Not enrolled in any classroom → a prompt to contact their teacher with no content available. Enrolled in an archived classroom → no content from that classroom while it is archived. To change what a student can access: add or remove them from classrooms via School portal → Classrooms → the classroom → Students tab.

heading: First login and changing your password
body: Your teacher or School Admin creates your account and emails you a temporary password. The first time you log in you must change that password before you can access any content. Steps: open the link in your welcome email, enter your school email and the temporary password, you are immediately taken to a Change Password screen, enter a new permanent password (at least 12 characters) and confirm it, click Save password. You cannot skip the password change screen — all portal pages redirect back to Change Password until it is completed. If you forget your password later, use the Forgot password link on the login page — a reset link is sent to your school email.

heading: Accessing your lessons
body: After logging in, the home screen shows all the subjects your teacher has assigned to your classroom. Your content is determined entirely by your classroom enrolment — you see only what your teacher has assigned. To access lessons: from the home screen click a subject card (e.g. Mathematics, Science), you see a list of units for that subject, click a unit to open it, click Start lesson to begin reading. Your progress saves automatically — you can leave and come back at any time. If your home screen shows no subjects or says No content available, your teacher may not have assigned a curriculum package to your classroom yet — contact your teacher.

heading: Taking quizzes
body: Each unit has one or more quizzes. You can attempt a quiz as many times as you like — only your best score is kept. Steps: open a unit and click Start quiz, read each question and select your answer, click Submit when you have answered all questions, your score is shown immediately with the correct answers highlighted, to try again click Retake quiz. Each attempt is recorded. Your best score out of all attempts appears in your progress summary and in your teacher's reports.

heading: Audio content — listening to lessons
body: Lessons have an audio version — a spoken reading of the lesson text. This is useful if you prefer to listen rather than read, or if you want to study while doing something else. To listen: open a unit and start the lesson, click the Play audio button (speaker icon) at the top of the lesson, the audio plays in your browser — use the pause, rewind, and speed controls in the audio player. Audio plays directly in the portal — no download needed. Listening to the audio counts as a lesson view for your progress. If the Play audio button is absent, no audio has been generated for that lesson yet.

heading: Your progress and streaks
body: The progress section shows your completion rate, quiz scores, and streak across all your enrolled classrooms. Completion rate is the percentage of units you have opened at least once across all assigned packages. Quiz average is the average of your best score per quiz across all units — only the best attempt per quiz counts. Streak is the number of consecutive days you have studied — opening a lesson or answering a quiz question keeps it alive, and it resets if a full day passes with no activity. Last active is the most recent date you did anything — lesson, quiz, or tutorial. To view your progress: click Progress in the navigation bar.

heading: Find your lessons
body: Your home screen shows the subjects your teacher has assigned to your classroom. Each subject contains units, and each unit has a lesson, a quiz, and sometimes a tutorial or experiment. To find lessons: log in to the student portal, your home screen lists all assigned subjects, click a subject card (e.g. Mathematics, Physical Science), the subject opens to show its list of units, use the search bar to filter units by name or topic. You see only the subjects assigned to your classroom — other platform content is not visible to you.

heading: Open a unit and read a lesson
body: Each unit has a lesson (text and diagrams), a quiz, and may have a tutorial or experiment. You can work through them in any order. Steps: click a unit name from the subject list, the unit page shows four tabs: Lesson, Quiz, Tutorial, and (if applicable) Experiment, click Lesson to read and scroll to see the full text, diagrams and charts are embedded in the lesson and zoom on click. Your progress saves automatically as you scroll — no save button needed. Opening the lesson counts as a view, which is recorded in your progress. You can leave and return — the lesson always opens where you left off.

heading: No content showing — what to do
body: If your home screen shows no subjects or a No content available message, there are several possible causes. No content available means your teacher has not assigned a curriculum package to your classroom yet — contact your teacher. Empty home screen (no subject cards) means you are not enrolled in any classroom — contact your teacher. Units greyed out or locked means your teacher set a sequence order and earlier units must be completed first — complete earlier units. A subject that disappeared means your teacher removed the package or archived the classroom — contact your teacher.

heading: Listen to the audio version of a lesson
body: Every lesson has a pre-recorded audio version. Use it to study while commuting or if you find it easier to absorb content by listening. Steps: open a unit and go to the Lesson tab, click the Play audio button (speaker icon) near the top of the lesson, the audio player appears with controls to play, pause, skip 10 seconds, or change playback speed (0.75x, 1x, 1.25x, 1.5x), you can read along with the text while the audio plays. Audio plays directly in the browser — no app or download needed. Listening counts as a lesson view for your completion rate. If the Play audio button is absent, no audio has been generated for that lesson.

heading: How to take a quiz
body: Each unit has one or more quizzes. You can start a quiz at any time — you do not need to finish the lesson first. Steps: open a unit and click the Quiz tab, if there are multiple quiz sets choose which one to take, click Start quiz, read each question carefully and select one answer per question, you can change your answer before submitting by clicking a different option, when you have answered every question click Submit. Your answers are submitted and your score is calculated immediately. You cannot submit a quiz with unanswered questions — the Submit button stays greyed out until all questions have an answer selected.

heading: Quiz results — reading your score
body: The results page appears immediately after you submit. It shows your score and the correct answer for every question. Your score is shown as a percentage and as a fraction (e.g. 7 out of 10). Each question is listed with: the question text, your answer (highlighted green if correct, red if wrong), and the correct answer if you got it wrong. An explanation is shown for any question you got wrong — read these carefully as they often contain the key concept from the lesson. Even if you scored well, review the ones marked wrong before retaking.

heading: Retake a quiz to improve your score
body: There is no limit on how many times you can retake a quiz. Each attempt is recorded separately. Only your best score is shown in your progress summary and reported to your teacher. Steps: from the results page click Retake quiz, the quiz starts again from the first question (questions may appear in a different order each time), submit when done, your new score is compared with all previous attempts and only the best is kept. Retaking a quiz can only improve your recorded score — it can never replace a good score with a worse one.

heading: How streaks work
body: A streak counts how many consecutive calendar days you have studied. Doing any learning activity — opening a lesson, answering a quiz question, or completing a tutorial — keeps your streak alive for that day. Actions that keep your streak alive: opening and reading a lesson (yes), answering at least one quiz question (yes), completing a tutorial (yes). Actions that do NOT keep it alive: logging in but not opening any content (no). A full calendar day passing with no activity resets your streak to 0. The streak resets at midnight in the school's local timezone. If you study at 11:55 PM one night and then again at 12:05 AM the next morning, both days count.

heading: What your teacher can see about your progress
body: Your teacher has a reports view that shows your progress. They can see: your completion rate (how many units you have opened divided by total units assigned), your quiz average (average of your best score per quiz across all quizzes), your current streak (consecutive days with any activity), your last active date (the most recent day you did anything in the portal), and an at-risk flag if you have had no activity for 7 or more days in a row (this clears automatically when you study again). Your teacher cannot see the specific answers you gave on a quiz — only your score. They also cannot see what you typed in tutorial exercises. Your individual responses are private.
